title: Stack and packaging: the proven claude-agents cockpit stack (Lovable-seeded), app lives in work-hq/, root package.json exposes onboard/dev scripts
context: ADR-P6-001 committed to a local app; this decides its construction. The claude-agents cockpit (Vite + TanStack Start + React 19 + Tailwind v4 + shadcn/ui) already solves the exact mechanics this app needs: import.meta.glob loading with HMR on file save, server functions for local file writes (save-human-input), the gather/copy dialog, status badges, and entity views for decisions/questions. The Personal OS template repo currently has no package.json at all; the packaging choice determines what a fresh clone looks like and how intrusive the code is to the markdown-first repo.

options.label: A
options.summary: Fork the claude-agents cockpit and prune: same stack, same layout, delete BMAD-only entities, add ritual screens and markdown contract-parsing
pros: Fastest credible path — the hard mechanics (glob loader + zod, server-fn writes, HMR, copy bridge, theming) arrive working, and the architect's match-existing-patterns rule points here: the claude-agents cockpit IS the established in-house pattern
Decisions/Questions views port almost unchanged and immediately render this phase's ADRs/OQs
Markdown loading fits the same mechanism: import.meta.glob with '?raw' gives contract files the identical HMR-on-save behaviour the JSON entities enjoy
Two repos, one mental model — maintenance knowledge transfers both ways
cons: Heavy dependency tree (~60 packages incl. full Radix/shadcn set) for a template whose ethos is minimalism; pruning discipline required
TanStack Start is still a fast-moving framework — upgrade churn lands on the template maintainer
Forking copies today's code, not a shared library: fixes in one app don't flow to the other automatically

options.label: B
options.summary: Minimal purpose-built app: Vite + React SPA with a tiny Hono/Express file API, hand-rolled CSS, no UI kit
pros: Small dependency surface — closest in spirit to the template's minimalism
No framework-beta risk; everything in the tree is boring and stable
cons: Rebuilds solved problems from scratch: routing, server file-writes, HMR-coupled data loading, dialogs, toasts — weeks of work to reach where the fork starts, with new bugs the fork has already shaken out
Two processes (SPA + API) or custom Vite middleware — more moving parts for the end user's npm run dev, not fewer
Diverges from the in-house pattern, so every future contributor context-switches between two unlike codebases

options.label: C
pros: Mainstream framework with long-horizon stability and abundant documentation
Server actions cover the file-write needs natively
cons: No in-house precedent — abandons the established cockpit pattern and its ported components for no capability gain
Heavier install and slower cold dev-start than Vite for a purely local tool
Next's cache/rendering machinery is dead weight for an app whose data source is the local filesystem with HMR

decision: Adopt Option A — same stack, different seed than originally drafted: rather than forking claude-agents/cockpit and pruning BMAD views, the UI was designed in Lovable against this stack (Vite + TanStack Start + React 19 + Tailwind v4 + shadcn/ui) from a design brief, then embedded into work-hq/ and de-Lovabled (sandbox build wrapper replaced with the plain Vite config mirroring claude-agents/cockpit; Lovable error-reporting hooks removed). The prototype ships all ADR-P6-005 screens plus the onboarding wizard, seeded from src/lib/mock.ts with localStorage persistence — the file-backed loaders (ADR-P6-002) and server-write input bridge (ADR-P6-003) replace mock.ts/localStorage as the next implementation step. Packaging: the repo gains a minimal root package.json whose only scripts delegate — "dev": "npm --prefix work-hq run dev", "onboard": "npm --prefix work-hq run onboard", "start" aliasing dev — plus a preflight hint when work-hq/node_modules is missing ('run npm install --prefix work-hq'). The onboard script sets an env flag so the server opens /onboarding. Node ≥ 20 is the documented prerequisite. .gitignore adds work-hq/node_modules, work-hq/dist, and docs/data/local/.
consequences: The template maintainer owns Work HQ dependency upgrades on a quarterly cadence (same rhythm as the skills review); users receive them via git pull + npm install. Fork divergence from the claude-agents cockpit is accepted and expected — shared-library extraction is explicitly deferred until both apps prove stable shapes (revisit only if a third such app appears). The root package.json is the first code artefact in the repo root; README explains in one line that it exists solely to launch the Work HQ and the OS works without it. Per OQ-002 (resolved), the README's setup section is written to be executed by the user's AI assistant — explicit, ordered, copy-runnable steps (check/install Node ≥ 20, npm install --prefix work-hq, npm run onboard) so a non-technical user can say 'set this up for me' and point their assistant at the README; per OQ-003 it also states that the as-is configuration assumes GitHub Copilot in VS Code native chat, with guidance for adapting to another assistant. Complexity tier: Moderate — the fork lands working, the wizard and contract parser are the two genuinely new subsystems.
costCapacity: No recurring or metered cost: no hosting, no CI change in this phase (Work HQ tests run locally; adding them to any future CI must pass the infra-cost-guard lens then). One-time ~250 MB node_modules per opted-in clone.
